content.full_text:
November 15, 1977

Robert Burt
Oil Fort Manufactures' Association
P.O. Box 1138
Sacramento, CA 95800

Dear Mr. Burt:

This letter confirms our telephone conversation of
November 9, 1977, concerning lobbyist employer report filed
by members of the Association.

As I understand the facts, several companies donated
funds to cover the costs of an air pollution control association
meeting. The funds totaled $1,000. The companies reported
the donations on lobbyist employer forms filed during the
summer of 1977, but they did not disclose the names of the
beneficiaries at that time because a list of persons attending the meeting had not been compiled. Such a list has now
been prepared.

On the basis of these facts I advised you that the
corporations should submit the list with a cover letter to the
Secretary of State. The cover letter should state that the
list supplements the previous filing and shall indicate the
date of the previous lobbyist employer report was filed.

I hope this information is of assistance.

Sincerely,

Natalie J. West
Staff Counsel
Legal Division

NJW:jo

December 21, 1977


Patricia Cain
Aero Vironment, Inc.
145 Vista Avenue
Pasadena, California  91107

Dear Ms. Cain:

     Enclosed is a copy of the letter sent, following their telephone conversation, by Natalie West of our staff to Robert Dart of the California Manufacturers Association. As you can see the advice given referred only to expenditures by members of the Association that employ lobbyists.

     Persons, including your company, whose payments to influence legislative or administrative action at the state level total $250 or more in any month have an obligation under Government Code Section 86108(b) to report those payments. However, this requirement does not apply if all of the payments made by the person are in the form of payments to benefit state officials or members of their immediate families (Government Code Section 82045(c)).

     As you have described your participation in the APCA meeting, all of your payments were in the nature of those covered from the reporting requirements as noted above. Consequently, you had no obligation to report the expenditure, and there was no need to submit a list of the beneficiaries of that expenditure.

                                        Sincerely yours,


                                        John Gianello
                                        Political Reform Analyst

JG:jw

Enclosure

sections.question_synthetic: Whether corporations that donated funds to an air pollution control association meeting must disclose the names of beneficiaries on lobbyist employer reports, and whether payments for such charitable/benefit activities require reporting under Government Code Section 86108(b).
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Corporations that donated funds to benefit state officials or members of their immediate families are exempt from reporting requirements under Government Code Section 82045(c), and therefore had no obligation to report the expenditure or submit a list of beneficiaries.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains two separate advice letters; no formal QUESTION/CONCLUSION sections with headers, but clear advisory content present. First letter (Burt) advises submitting supplemental list with cover letter; second letter (Cain) clarifies exemption under Government Code Section 82045(c) for payments benefiting state officials.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether corporations that donated funds to an air pollution control association meeting must disclose the names of beneficiaries on lobbyist employer reports, and whether payments for such charitable/benefit activities require reporting under Government Code Section 86108(b).

CONCLUSION: Corporations that donated funds to benefit state officials or members of their immediate families are exempt from reporting requirements under Government Code Section 82045(c), and therefore had no obligation to report the expenditure or submit a list of beneficiaries.

embedding.summary: This document contains two FPPC advice letters from 1977 addressing whether companies must report donations to an air pollution control association meeting and disclose beneficiaries on lobbyist employer forms. The FPPC advises that such donations, when made to benefit state officials or their families, are exempt from reporting requirements.
